issue_title: Open files with O_EXCL in Python 2
issue_body:
`io.open` supports taking file descriptor instead of a file path (and can take ownership of the fd). `os.open` lets you open a file in exclusive mode and returns a file descriptor. You could do something like the following to open the file in the mode specified.

```
diff --git a/fs/osfs.py b/fs/osfs.py
index e53e5e2..9b2c330 100644
--- a/fs/osfs.py
+++ b/fs/osfs.py
@@ -422,8 +422,8 @@ class OSFS(FS):
         _path = self.validatepath(path)
         sys_path = self._to_sys_path(_path)
         with convert_os_errors('open', path):
-            if six.PY2 and _mode.exclusive and self.exists(path):
-                raise FileExists(path)
+            if six.PY2 and _mode.exclusive:
+                sys_path = os.open(sys_path, os.O_CREAT | os.O_EXCL)
             _encoding = encoding or 'utf-8'
             return io.open(
                 sys_path,
```

*(I tried running the tests to see what this breaks, but for some reason tox wouldn't stop barfing at me even on regular v2.0.24 without this change.)*

I've noticed a minor issue in the documentation. The OSFS create_mode parameter is not mentioned:
https://pyfilesystem2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/osfs.html

Also, congrats on 2.0!

issue_title: pynwb.base.TimeSeries.starting_time & rate does not return the floats given in the argument
issue_body:
## 1) Bug
I am able to create a TimeSeries object but while checking if the object was what I intended to create I noticed something weird.
If `ts = TimeSeries (...)` (as shown below), I can get the exact **float** values I gave in the argument for `ts.resolution` and `ts.conversion`.
But for `ts.starting_time` and `ts.rate`, they do not return floats instead they return **None**; which is the pynwb default value.

### Steps to Reproduce
```
from pynwb import TimeSeries

ts = TimeSeries( name = "dummy",
                 source = "soma",
                 data = list(range(0, 100, 10)),
                 unit = "milliVolt",
                 resolution = 0.01,
                 conversion = 1000.0,
                 timestamps = list(range(10)),
                 starting_time = 0.0,
                 rate = 1/0.01 )

>> ts.resolution # same for ts.conversion
0.01

>> type(ts.conversion) # same for ts.resolution
<type 'float'> 

>> ts.starting_time # same for ts.rate
None

>> type(ts.rate) # same for ts.starting_time
<type 'NoneType'>
```
### Environment
Please describe your environment according to the following bullet points.
- **Python Executable:** Python
- **Python Version:** Python 2.7
- **Operating System:**  Ubuntu 16.04
- **Pynwb Version:**  0.4.1

The following are making my npm install take **literally** more than 30 minutes on not-that great connection as it does clone the full repo:
```
  "source-code-pro": "git://github.com/adobe-fonts/source-code-pro.git#release",
   "source-sans-pro": "git://github.com/adobe-fonts/source-sans-pro.git#release",
```

Can we avoid that ?
For placement on the classic notebook tree view

issue_title: Regression: mapped (foreign-key) columns not numpy compatible!
issue_body:
Attn: @llllllllll - commit 689894ccb0bca5687ef0bd9ca39ecf0c80cd747b introduced a regression whereby no foreign-key columns can pass through odo.

**Repro:**
```python
# setup and populate a db with a foreign-key
from itertools import chain

import blaze as bz
import numpy as np
from numpy.random import choice, randint
from odo import odo
import pandas as pd
import sqlalchemy as sa

engine = sa.create_engine('sqlite://')
metadata = sa.MetaData(bind=engine)
T1 = sa.Table(
    'demo',
    metadata,
    sa.Column('timestamp', sa.String, primary_key=True),
    sa.Column('label_id', sa.SmallInteger, sa.ForeignKey("labels.pkid"), nullable=False),
)
T2 = sa.Table(
    'labels',
    metadata,
    sa.Column('pkid', sa.SmallInteger, primary_key=True),
    sa.Column('label', sa.String(1), nullable=False),
)

metadata.create_all()


labels = pd.DataFrame(
    list(zip(range(4), ['A','B','C','D'])),
    columns=['pkid', 'label']
)
odo(labels, T2)
```
Attempting to pull out the data gives a not numpy-compatible error:
```python
In [3]: odo(bz.data(T1).head(), pd.DataFrame)
Traceback (most recent call last):

  File "<ipython-input-3-310b6f62f8be>", line 1, in <module>
    odo(bz.data(T).head(), pd.DataFrame)

  File "c:\dev\packages\odo\odo\odo.py", line 91, in odo
    return into(target, source, **kwargs)

  File "C:\Python\lib\site-packages\multipledispatch\dispatcher.py", line 164, in __call__
    return func(*args, **kwargs)

  File "c:\dev\packages\blaze\blaze\interactive.py", line 406, in into
    return into(a, result, **kwargs)

  File "C:\Python\lib\site-packages\multipledispatch\dispatcher.py", line 164, in __call__
    return func(*args, **kwargs)

  File "c:\dev\packages\odo\odo\into.py", line 43, in wrapped
    return f(*args, **kwargs)

  File "c:\dev\packages\odo\odo\into.py", line 53, in into_type
    return convert(a, b, dshape=dshape, **kwargs)

  File "c:\dev\packages\odo\odo\core.py", line 44, in __call__
    return _transform(self.graph, *args, **kwargs)

  File "c:\dev\packages\odo\odo\core.py", line 60, in _transform
    x = f(x, excluded_edges=excluded_edges, **kwargs)

  File "c:\dev\packages\odo\odo\backends\sql.py", line 698, in select_or_selectable_to_frame
    dtypes[field] = dtype.to_numpy_dtype()

  File "C:\Python\lib\site-packages\datashape\coretypes.py", line 173, in to_numpy_dtype
    raise TypeError('DataShape %s is not NumPy-compatible' % self)

TypeError: DataShape map[int16, {pkid: int16, label: string[1]}] is not NumPy-compatible
```
...in the previous commit this worked as expected:
```python
In [3]: odo(bz.data(T1).head(), pd.DataFrame)
Out[3]: 
             timestamp  label_id
0  2017-01-01 00:00:00         2
1  2017-01-02 00:00:00         2
2  2017-01-03 00:00:00         1
3  2017-01-04 00:00:00         2
4  2017-01-05 00:00:00         3
5  2017-01-06 00:00:00         1
6  2017-01-07 00:00:00         0
7  2017-01-08 00:00:00         2
8  2017-01-09 00:00:00         2
9  2017-01-10 00:00:00         1
```

**Note:** In the previous (*"good"*) commit this only works if I use `bz.data(T1).head()` or take a slice `bz.data(T1)[:]` - in both commits `odo(bz.data(T1), pd.DataFrame)`  will fail with the not numpy-compatible error. AFAICS this has something to do with the path it takes where the latter goes through chunked dataframes whereas the slice/head uses a selectable as far as I can tell.

The odo documentation is quite sparse. It does not explain some basic concepts like how to pass further parameters.  For example, when loading a csv file, how do I tell odo that the file is latin1 encoded?

data=[]
odo('mylatin1.csv',data)

If you can explain the basic premise to me, I'd be happy to make the change and submit a pull request

issue_labels: [{'id': 865017416, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4NjUwMTc0MTY=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pytroll/satpy/labels/component:scene', 'name': 'component:scene', 'color': '1d76db', 'default': False}, {'id': 323805264, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwzMjM4MDUyNjQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pytroll/satpy/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': True}, {'id': 751601587, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3NTE2MDE1ODc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pytroll/satpy/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '5319e7', 'default': True}, {'id': 323805265, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwzMjM4MDUyNjU=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pytroll/satpy/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
The Scene object was built and designed to replacing existing interfaces coming from satpy's predecessor mpop. However, now that we have switched to using xarray.DataArray objects as our main data containers we could and probably should switch to using xarray.Dataset objects (http://xarray.pydata.org/en/stable/generated/xarray.Dataset.html).

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Add a `Scene.to_xarray_dataset` or maybe `Scene.to_dataset` method that returns an xarray Dataset object from the loaded datasets.

**Describe any changes to existing user workflow**
Additional optional method right now that doesn't need to be used for anything. We *could* use this in the future to separate out the Scene -> writer interface so that writers are more xarray-specific (read: generic) and not Scene specific.

**Additional python or other dependencies**
None

**Describe any changes required to the build process**
None

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
It has been on my radar for a while that we could/should replace the Scene object with a Dataset object, but right now there is too much functionality inside the Scene object to do this smoothly.

issue_title: ArgSplitter uses final argument for all subjobs
issue_body:
When submitting jobs using `ArgSplitter`, all subjobs are submitted with the arguments of the final job (`[109]` in the example).

### Example:
```bash
echo '#!/bin/bash' > example.sh
echo 'date >> Example.${1}.txt' >> example.sh
echo 'echo ${1} >> Example.${1}.txt' >> example.sh
echo 'cat "Example.${1}.txt"' >> example.sh
chmod +x example.sh
```
```python
j = Job()
j.application = Executable(exe=File('example.sh'))
j.splitter = ArgSplitter(args=[[i] for i in range(100, 110)])
j.outputfiles = [DiracFile(namePattern='Example.*.txt')]
j.backend = Dirac()
j.submit()
```

Follow up to the "Failure to upload output data from Executable job" thread on `lhcb-distributed-anaysis`.

issue_title: setup.py should be more informative on OS X
issue_body:
I've recently been trying to compile cpptraj/pytraj on OS X and ran into an issue that took me some time to track down. After getting Macports/cython etc all set up, I built libcpptraj and then tried to make pytraj. I first got hit with this error:
```
libcpptraj was NOT detected to be installed with openmp. You can recompile it with -openmp flag or
disable openpm install in pytraj by adding --disable-openmp
```
Fair enough - so I went back and re-configured libcpptraj with OpenMP. After that build finished I once again tried to install pytraj. Ended up with a new error:
```
...
8 warnings generated.
clang++ -bundle -undefined dynamic_lookup -L/opt/local/lib -Wl,-headerpad_max_install_names -L/opt/local/lib/db48 build/temp.macosx-10.11-x86_64-2.7/pytraj/c_dict.o -L/Users/test/GitHub/cpptraj/lib/ -lcpptraj -o build/lib.macosx-10.11-x86_64-2.7/pytraj/c_dict.so -O0 -ggdb -fopenmp
ld: library not found for -lgomp
clang: error: linker command failed with exit code 1 (use -v to see invocation)
error: command 'clang++' failed with exit status 1
```
What I found strange was that instead of gcc (my active port) pytraj setup was defaulting to clang. This persisted even when I tried to explicitly set the CC/CXX environment variables. After some digging, I found it was due to this part of setup.py:
```python
if sys.platform == 'darwin':
    # copied from ParmEd
    # You *need* to use clang and clang++ for extensions on a Mac;
    # Anaconda does annoying stuff that breaks this, since their distutils
    # automatically tries to use "gcc", which would conflict with the MacPorts
    # gcc... sigh.
    os.environ['CXX'] = 'clang++'
    os.environ['CC'] = 'clang'
```
So clang is a silent requirement to build on OS X. The problem is that the default clang does not support OpenMP (@swails I think maybe the macports one does?), but requiring OpenMP is the default for pytraj. I think that pytraj's setup.py should be a bit more informative and state that clang is required on OSX, and disable the OpenMP requirement on OS X (with a warning) if the active clang does not support it. @hainm What do you think?

issue_title: Fuzzy marching errors with trailing slashes
issue_body: If a WARC file contains records assigned to a URL that ends with a `/`, pywb will serve that record even when that trailing `/` is missing, without forwarding to the correct URL. In the case of index pages this leads to images and other resources addressed with relative URLs to request the wrong resources. ### Resource URL of resource: `http://test.co.br/folder/` HTML code: ```HTML ``` ### Requests Request URI: `http://test.co.br/folder/` → Image requested from: `http://test.co.br/folder/images/test.png` ✅ nice! Request URI: `http://test.co.br/folder/` → Image requested from: `http://test.co.br/images/test.png` 💀 wrong relative URL!!

issue_title: In-Memory cache fills up indefinitely by default
issue_body: Right now, the in-memory cache only ever gets fuller. When pulling a lot of data from the API, this can start to be a problem. I'd propose either explicitly mentioning this and how to reset the cache in documentation, or (ideally) having a maximum size for the cache / maximum number of stored objects, with some expulsion policy (LRU?), and a sane default value to start off with (but this might be hard to do properly).

issue_title: As an API user, a call to update an Publisher generates a Task
issue_labels: [{'id': 294019786, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/PulpQE/pulp-smash/labels/Issue%20Type:%20Test%20Case', 'name': 'Issue Type: Test Case', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 639323840, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/PulpQE/pulp-smash/labels/Pulp%20Version:%203', 'name': 'Pulp Version: 3', 'color': 'd93f0b', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Related issue:
https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2409

As part of changes required to adjust Pulp-Smash to fit Pulp3 needs, it will be necessary to adjust the callback function, `response_handler`. These functions are used together with `api.Client` mainly.

Pulp2 and Pulp3 differ in the way the responses for certain actions are sent back. 

In Pulp3 the update of a data in a certain repository will generate the follow **202** response.

```json
{
    "_href": "http://pulp3.dev:8000/api/v3/tasks/4fc2e1fe-2564-4818-9b0d-4116a32ec2d0/",
    "task_id": "4fc2e1fe-2564-4818-9b0d-4116a32ec2d0"
}
```

Using the` api.json_handler` as response_handler to handle the previous response, it will generate an error, since it is not ready to parse this pattern of response. 

Error will occur in the function [poll_spawned_tasks](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/394d3510d052660d8ed378cc919861a18e2f7400/pulp_smash/api.py#L346), and [poll_task](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/394d3510d052660d8ed378cc919861a18e2f7400/pulp_smash/api.py#L371)

There are basically 2 choices, create other functions to handle Pulp3 response, or adapt the current ones to verify what version of Pulp is under test and perform different steps as needed.

Possibly changes to CLI Client`s callbacks will be required as well, but not identified at the moment.

Example Pulp 2 task:

```json
{
 "_href": "/pulp/api/v2/tasks/0fe4fcab-a040-11e1-a71c-00508d977dff/",
 "state": "running",
 "worker_name": "reserved_resource_worker-0@your.domain.com",
 "task_id": "0fe4fcab-a040-11e1-a71c-00508d977dff",
 "task_type": "pulp.server.tasks.repository.sync_with_auto_publish",
 "progress_report": {"contents vary": "depending on operation"},
 "result": null,
 "start_time": "2012-05-17T16:48:00Z",
 "finish_time": null,
 "exception": null,
 "traceback": null,
 "tags": [
   "pulp:repository:f16",
   "pulp:action:sync"
 ],
 "spawned_tasks": [{"href": "/pulp/api/v2/tasks/7744e2df-39b9-46f0-bb10-feffa2f7014b/",
                    "task_id": "7744e2df-39b9-46f0-bb10-feffa2f7014b" }],
 "error": null
}
```

Example Pulp 3 task:

```json
"results": [{
  "_href": "http://pulp3.dev:8000/api/v3/tasks/e99435f5-0ba4-4ccf-bb66-60a1bd120ae1/",
  "group": null,
  "state": "completed",
  "started_at": "2017-10-20T18:37:47.630237Z",
  "finished_at": "2017-10-20T18:37:47.684939Z",
  "non_fatal_errors": [],
  "error": null,
  "worker": "http://pulp3.dev:8000/api/v3/workers/reserved_resource_worker_2@pulp3.dev/",
  "parent": null,
  "tags": [],
  "progress_reports": []
}]
```

issue_title: Provide decomposed RPM names in pulp_smash/constants.py
issue_body:
Currently the name, epoch, version, release, and arch are encoded in the pulp_smash.constants.RPM and pulp_smash.constants.RPM2 constants, but not easily accessible. Some tests hard code in this data as a work around:

See:
* https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/tests/rpm/api_v2/test_content_applicability.py#L25
* https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/tests/rpm/api_v2/test_updateinfo.py#L100

To avoid this in the future, it has been suggested to instead provide this in a dicitonary in pulp_smash/constants.py and then concatenate the contents to form the full RPM name so only one reference will have to be updated in the future.

For reference: [RPM naming convention](http://ftp.rpm.org/max-rpm/ch-rpm-file-format.html)

Roughly it would look like this:
```python
# file: pulp_smash/constants.py
from types import MappingProxyType

RPM_DATA = MappingProxyType({
    'name' : 'bear',
     'epoch' : None,
     'version': '4.1',
     'release' : 1,
     'arch' : 'noarch',
     'vendor' : None,
 })

RPM  = '{}-{}{}-{}-{}.rpm'.format(
        RPM_DATA['name'],
        RPM_DATA['epoch'] + '!' if RPM_DATA['epoch'] != None else '',
        RPM_DATA['version'],
        RPM_DATA['release'],
        RPM_DATA['arch'],
 )
```

issue_title: v1.0: Lazily load model class in Field and relay.ConnectionField as in v0.x
issue_body:
Currently rewriting our GraphQL API to work with graphene 1.0 but I'm coming across a big problem:

In order to create the models, I've written a construction class that will convert the actual database models to models used by graphene. There arises a problem however when creating relations on the models using the Field and relay.ConnectionField classes. In graphene 0.x I would be able to provide the name string as argument to Field and relay.ConnectionField, but apparently this is not possible anymore in 1.0 (I'm wondering why you took this out as well). 

It's impossible to import the model classes in the construction class due to circular dependencies. I'm hoping there is another solution to this problem, other than completely rewriting everything to something that is not logically structured at all anymore. Is this something that is going to change in the future or is there a fix available?

We update the version with the "dev" indicator after publishing a release, but we sometimes forget to do so in a timely manner. Other PRs get merged, before the "dev" indicator is in place. This doesn't really impact any actual release, but it does lead to misnamed/misordered packages in testpypi:

* https://test.pypi.org/project/watchmaker/#history

We ought to be able to avoid this by changing how we add the "build" to the version, in .travis.yml. I.e. also inject the "dev" indicator at the same time.

* https://github.com/plus3it/watchmaker/blob/develop/.travis.yml#L68
pip-compile created my requirements and used version 1.1.0. However, on some of my servers that version no longer exists.

    test acc:  [0.56000000238418579]
    y:         [0.0, 1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 1.0]
    pred:      [0.0, 0.98, 0.0, 0.98, 0.0, 0.99, 0.95, 0.0, 0.72, 0.99]
    y sum:     56.0

The net has clearly learned the function, but the accuracy metric does not show it. The accuracy results written out for Tensorboard are similarly incorrect, showing a constant 0.56 for "BinaryAccuracy/Validation". (Again, 56 was the number of 1s in the target output in this case.)

    def bxo_accuracy_test(is_binary, n_epochs, tensorboard_dir, run_id):
        train_size = 1000
        test_size = 100
        X = np.random.rand(train_size + test_size, 5)
        prod = X.dot([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
        np.clip(prod, 0.0, None, prod)
        class_num = np.sign(prod)
        if is_binary:
            y = class_num.reshape((class_num.shape[0], 1))
        else:
            class_num_int = np.array(class_num, dtype=np.int)
            identity = np.identity(2)
            y = identity[class_num_int]
        X_train = X[:train_size]
        X_test = X[train_size:]
        y_train = y[:train_size]
        y_test = y[train_size:]
        
        tf.reset_default_graph()
        net = tl.input_data([None, X.shape[1]])
        net = tl.batch_normalization(net)
        net = tl.relu(net)
        net = tl.fully_connected(net, 100, weights_init='xavier')
        net = tl.batch_normalization(net)
        net = tl.relu(net)
        net = tl.fully_connected(net, 50, weights_init='xavier')
        net = tl.batch_normalization(net)
        net = tl.relu(net)
        net = tl.fully_connected(net, 25, weights_init='xavier')
        net = tl.batch_normalization(net)
        net = tl.relu(net)
        final_width = 1 if is_binary else 2
        net = tl.fully_connected(net, final_width, 'sigmoid', weight_decay=0.0)
        optimizer = tl.Momentum(0.01)
        loss = tl.binary_crossentropy if is_binary else tl.categorical_crossentropy
        net = tl.regression(net, optimizer=optimizer, loss=loss, metric='accuracy')
        model = tl.DNN(net, tensorboard_dir=tensorboard_dir)
        batch_size = 100
        with tf.Session():
            model.fit(X_train, y_train, n_epochs, (X_test, y_test), True, batch_size, run_id=run_id)
            pred = model.predict(X_test)
            print('train acc: ' + str(model.evaluate(X_train, y_train)))
            print('test acc:  ' + str(model.evaluate(X_test, y_test)))
            if is_binary:
                print('y:         ' + str([round(v[0], 2) for v in y_test[:10]]))
                print('pred:      ' + str([round(v[0], 2) for v in pred[:10]]))
                print('y sum:     ' + str(y_test.sum()))
            else:
                print('y:         ' + str([str([round(w, 2) for w in v]) for v in y_test[:10]]))
                print('pred:      ' + str([str([round(w, 2) for w in v]) for v in pred[:10]]))
                print('y sum:     ' + str(y_test.sum(axis=0)))

issue_title: history and list commands are largely redundant
issue_body:
The **history** and **list** commands largely have the same basic functionality of displaying commands from the history. 

There is a partial overlap in the functionality where they both support some of the same features.  But each also has at least one feature that the other doesn't have.

It would probably be more convenient for end users to combine all of the functionality into one common **history** command and remove the **list** command.

issue_title: Support importing an account via private key
issue_body: So people can use accounts that weren't initially created using Certbot.

With a fresh install of Nginx on Debian Jessie, I'm given a prompt like this when running Certbot with the Nginx plugin:
```
Which names would you like to activate HTTPS for?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: _
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Select the appropriate numbers separated by commas and/or spaces (Enter 'c' to
cancel):
```
This is because the Nginx config contains `server_name _;`, however, this designates a catch-all/default vhost and requesting a certificate for this name will fail.

More information on the possible values here we need to handle can be found [here](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/server_names.html#miscellaneous_names).

issue_title: Why does sphinx think asterisks are links?
issue_body: I made #4497 to fix [this](https://travis-ci.org/certbot/website/builds/221423692#L1275) but I don't understand why sphinx was interpreting things that way. I'm more worried about just getting the website repo fixed for now, but we should look into this.

issue_title: Doesn't work with applications which use absolute URLs
issue_body:
I've noticed that a few applications that I've tried to proxy recently use absolute urls.

For example when running on a regular server if you go to `http://localhost:1234/` it will look for a stylesheet at`/style/index.css`. This works fine when running locally but when using nbserverproxy I visit `http://localhost:8888/lab/proxy/1234` which still looks for styles at `/style/index.css` rather than `/lab/proxy/1234/style/index.css`.

These applications often take a `baseurl` config option somewhere which lets you prepend all urls with a base path, such as `/lab/proxy/1234`. This would allow me to fix this functionality. However it appears nbserverproxy rewrites its urls so when I visit `http://localhost:8888/lab/proxy/1234` the path gets rewritten to `/`. This then causes problems because the application is now expecting a base url of `/lab/proxy/1234/`. This results in the HTML page not displaying because flask or whatever doesn't have a route for `/`, but the CSS would display ok if it were ever requested.

issue_title: Build lists of registered tokens and open channels from events
EndpointRegistry.sol is very expensive to use
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

It seems that everytime Raiden is started the `register_endpoint` is called (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/d68cbe87381f7f949beb0cbf358638274aacad75/raiden/network/discovery.py#L74). This call to the smart contract is quite expensive and costs 500.000 gas. 

## Solution

There should be a way to not do this transaction so often and also the EndpointRegistry.sol should be reiterated to see if it can be improved.


## Tasklist

- [ ] Update the code so that it doesn't call EndpointRegistry.sol everytime Raiden is started
- [ ] Reiterate EndpointRegistry.sol to make it less expensive to interact with

I hope it's okay if I'm asking this here.

A lot of data sets (e.g. Enron) contain only plain text messages or no explicit information whether an email has been forwarded or replied. 

In order to reconstruct this information I was thinking about trying to recover nested messages e.g. (just an example)

```
Message-ID: <10792109.1075856435465.JavaMail.evans@thyme>

issue_title: Simplify code structure of components using Sympl helper functions
issue_body:
Currently the component objects used by CliMT (e.g. ClimtPrognostic) are fairly complex, in that they give user components inherited methods that aren't explicitly defined but will be used within their code. The objects also use multiple inheritance, which can be confusing to scientific programmers with less knowledge of object-oriented programming, and less readable even if they are knowledgeable.

Since Sympl added some helper functions for retrieving/storing numpy arrays in states, the functionality these structures currently give can be provided without any new classes. We should refactor the code to use this new style.

The vast majority of command groups send the help message when no subcommands are given - they do this with a statement like: ```py
if ctx.invoked_subcommand is None:
    await ctx.send_help()
```

This is really common and honestly, it's useless boilerplate. It gets worse when we have sub-groups, with people often using `isinstance` checks to send help there. 

@mikeshardmind came up with the suggestion to add this to the `group()` decorator instead, but optionally disable it with a flag in case automatically sending help on no invoked subcommand wasn't desired.

We realised this would introduce a somewhat breaking change - all of a sudden, 3rd party cogs who hadn't removed the call to `ctx.send_help()` would be getting duplicate help being sent. For this reason, we've proposed a grace period where the flag will default to `False`, and will be set to `True` for beta 17.

issue_title: Incompatibility with Python 3 versions lower than 3.5
issue_body:
It appears that the library is incompatible with Python versions lower than 3.5, despite claiming to be [fully Python 3 compatible](https://github.com/chainside/btcpy/blob/master/setup.py#L26).

The issue stems from the following idiom mainly:

```python
 m, *pubkeys, n = args
```

Unpacking rules were much stricter prior to Python 3.5 and did not allow this type of argument unpacking where a single argument follows star arguments.

In order for the library to be compatible with Python 3 versions lower than 3.5, it'd have to use the following idiom instead:

```python
 m, n, *pubkeys = args
```

This appears to happen in quite a few places. Python 3.4 compatibility would be nice because that version of Python 3 often comes preinstalled with many systems.

Reference: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0448/

issue_title: Give a better error when users make an async fixture non-function-scoped, or use it with a sync test
issue_body:
It would be nice if when Trio mode was activated, this gave an error:

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def my_async_fixture():
    ...

# Notice: not an async test
def test_something(my_async_fixture):
    ...
```

Obviously we can't do this when trio mode isn't activated, because someone else might be handling the fixture. But when it is, we could give a nice error in theory.

The blocker is that I'm not sure how we're supposed to check the pytest configuration from `pytest_fixture_setup`...

Also, it would also be nice if we could make it so when Trio mode is activated, this gives an error:

```python
@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
async def whatever():
    ...
```

This is tricky because if someone does do this, then from `pytest_setup_fixture`'s point of view, it actually *won't detect that this fixture should be treated as a trio fixture*, because for a session fixture we can't see which tests it will ultimately be used with (and detect that they have `@pytest.mark.trio`). So this actually ends up being similar to the above case.

(Unless there's some way to ask pytest for a list of all the items that a session-scoped fixture will ultimately be used for, but I don't know of one.)

If I have a form, like in:

    from splinter.browser import Browser
    browser = Browser()
    browser.visit('http://www.google.com/')
    my_form = browser.find_by_tag('form').first

the object `my_form._element` will have the `submit` method.

As we should not use `_element` since it is related to Selenium, Splinter should exposes a `submit` method in this form-object, so we can submit forms without needing to search for the submit button (or element that actually submits the form).

It is not useful when we want to test if some button, when clicked, submits the form correctly. But it's very useful when we just want to submit the form and check what is the response (and don't want to know the name of the button/element that submits the form).
